perturbation.original_text: This Agreement and performance thereunder shall be governed by the laws of the State of Delaware, excluding its conflicts of laws rules.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement and performance thereunder shall be governed by the laws of the State of Minnesota, excluding its conflicts of laws rules.
perturbation.explanation: The governing law clause has been changed from Delaware to Minnesota, which is where Northwest Airlines, Inc. is incorporated. This is relevant because the LLC is a Delaware entity, and contracts related to Delaware entities are normally governed by Delaware law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware law governs the internal affairs of Delaware corporations and LLCs. Specifying Minnesota law might create confusion regarding the interpretation and enforcement of the agreement, particularly regarding the LLC's structure and operation which are governed by Delaware law.

perturbation.justification: The changed text alters the governing law from Delaware to Minnesota. The Law Explanation states that 'Delaware law governs the internal affairs of Delaware corporations and LLCs.' Scraped Law Snippet 1, Delaware Code Title 8 Chapter 1, confirms Delaware's corporation law. By changing the governing law, the contract introduces a structural flaw by potentially applying non-Delaware law to a Delaware LLC, which contradicts the typical legal expectation and may lead to confusion or unenforceability, thus meeting the definition of a Structural Flaw Legal Contradiction. The Perturbation Explanation also supports this conclusion.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party agrees that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for breach of the confidentiality and other obligations of this Agreement. Accordingly, in addition to all other remedies that each Party may have, each Party shall be entitled to specific performance and injunctive relief or other equitable relief as determined by a court of competent jurisdiction as a remedy for any breach of this Agreement. Each Party agrees to waive any requirement for a bond in connection with any such injunctive or other equitable relief.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party agrees that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for breach of the confidentiality and other obligations of this Agreement. Accordingly, in addition to all other remedies that each Party may have, each Party shall be entitled to specific performance and injunctive relief or other equitable relief as determined by a court of competent jurisdiction as a remedy for any breach of this Agreement. Each Party agrees that they may or may not waive any requirement for a bond in connection with any such injunctive or other equitable relief.
perturbation.explanation: The waiver of bond requirement has been modified to be uncertain, creating ambiguity regarding whether a bond is required for injunctive relief. This introduces a contradiction within the clause itself.
perturbation.law_explanation: FRCP 65(c) addresses security (bond) requirements for preliminary injunctions or temporary restraining orders. Changing the bond waiver to be discretionary contradicts the purpose of clearly defining the conditions for injunctive relief. It undermines the certainty expected in legal agreements by introducing optionality where clarity is necessary.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 65. Injunctions and Restraining Orders | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 65. Injunctions and Restraining Orders (a) Preliminary Injunction. (1) Notice. The court may issue a preliminary injunction only on notice to the adverse party. (2) Consolidating the Hearing with the Trial on the Merits. Before or after beginning the hearing on a motion for a preliminary injunction, the court may advance the trial on the merits and consolidate it with the hearing. Even when consolidation is not ordered, evidence that is received on the motion and that would be admissible at trial becomes part of the trial record and need not be repeated at trial. But the court must preserve any party's right to a jury trial. (b) Temporary Restraining Order. (1) Issuing Without Notice. The court may issue a temporary restraining order without written or oral notice to the adverse party or its attorney only if: (A) specific facts in an affidavit or a verified complaint clearly show that immediate and irreparable injury, loss, or damage will result to the movant before the adverse party can be heard in opposition; and (B) the movant's attorney certifies in writing any efforts made to give notice and the reasons why it should not be required. (2) Contents; Expiration. Every temporary restraining order issued without notice must state the date and hour it was issued; describe the injury and state why it is irreparable; state why the order was issued without notice; and be promptly filed in the clerk's office and entered in the record. The order expires at the time after entry—not to exceed 14 days—that the court sets, unless before that time the court, for good cause, extends it for a like period or the adverse party consents to a longer extension. The reasons for an extension must be entered in the record. (3) Expediting the Preliminary-Injunction Hearing. If the order is issued without notice, the moti

Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The original text mandates a waiver of bond requirements, while the changed text introduces uncertainty by stating parties 'may or may not waive' this requirement. This creates a contradiction because the original agreement provides certainty regarding injunctive relief conditions, whereas the modified version undermines this certainty by allowing for optionality, specifically regarding a bond. Although the scraped snippets do not directly address bond waiver for injunctive relief, the Law Explanation indicates that FRCP 65(c) is relevant, as it pertains to security (bond) requirements for preliminary injunctions. Making the bond waiver discretionary contradicts the objective of clearly outlining the conditions for injunctive relief, indicating the presence of a structural flaw that leads to a legal contradiction, according to the given definition. Therefore, the modification introduces ambiguity and weakens the enforceability of the original clause.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement may be enforced by any Member of the Company with respect to any information or data regarding such Member disclosed by the Investor m violation of this Agreement. Except as set forth in the preceding sentence, nothing in this Agreement shall provide any benefit to any third party or entitle any third party to any claim, cause of action, remedy or right of any kind, it being the intent of the Parties that this Agreement shall not be construed as a third party beneficiary contract.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement may be enforced by any Member of the Company with respect to any information or data regarding such Member disclosed by the Investor m violation of this Agreement. Except as set forth in the preceding sentence, nothing in this Agreement shall provide any benefit to any third party except for the CEO who may enforce the agreement, it being the intent of the Parties that this Agreement shall not be construed as a third party beneficiary contract.
perturbation.explanation: The third-party beneficiary clause has been modified to provide an exception for the CEO. This undermines the clear intent of the original clause to exclude third-party beneficiaries, creating potential ambiguity and legal challenges.
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 302 defines intended and incidental beneficiaries. By explicitly naming the CEO as an exception, the modification creates an 'intended beneficiary' status for the CEO, directly conflicting with the statement that 'this Agreement shall not be construed as a third party beneficiary contract.' This inconsistency complicates the enforceability of the agreement by potentially granting rights to a party the original contract intended to exclude.

perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly states that the agreement should not be construed as a third-party beneficiary contract. The changed text creates an exception for the CEO, designating them as a beneficiary with enforcement rights. According to the Law Explanation, this creates an 'intended beneficiary' status, directly contradicting the initial denial of third-party beneficiary status. Since the structural flaw creates an explicitly contradictory statement within the legal document, the contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement may not be terminated except following Withdrawal by the Investor pursuant to the terms of the LLC Agreement. The obligations of confidentiality and limitations on use under this Agreement shall survive termination of this Agreement for a period of five (5) years from the execution of this Agreement; PROVIDED,
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement may not be terminated except following Withdrawal by the Investor pursuant to the terms of the LLC Agreement. The obligations of confidentiality and limitations on use under this Agreement shall terminate if the CEO declares it to be so. The obligations of confidentiality and limitations on use under this Agreement shall survive termination of this Agreement for a period of five (5) years from the execution of this Agreement; PROVIDED,
perturbation.explanation: Introducing a condition that the CEO can terminate the confidentiality agreement introduces an exception that conflicts with the stated termination condition linked to the LLC Agreement. This creates ambiguity and undermines the enforceability of the confidentiality provisions.
perturbation.law_explanation: UTSA § 2(b) pertains to the duration of injunctive relief for misappropriation of trade secrets. Allowing the CEO to unilaterally terminate the confidentiality agreement conflicts with the potential need for long-term protection of trade secrets, especially if misappropriation occurs. This contradicts the intended protection under trade secret law by introducing an arbitrary termination condition.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a condition where the CEO can terminate the confidentiality agreement, directly conflicting with the original condition linked to the LLC Agreement. This undermines the enforceability of the confidentiality provisions. The LLM explanation states that allowing the CEO to unilaterally terminate the confidentiality agreement conflicts with the potential need for long-term protection of trade secrets, potentially violating the UTSA. Although the scraped snippets do not directly address the UTSA or confidentiality agreements, the Perturbation Explanation and LLM Explanation indicate that the altered clause introduces ambiguity which constitutes a structural flaw-legal contradiction.
